claim.statement: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) is a robust phenomenon: students attending high-ability schools have lower academic self-concepts than similarly able peers in less competitive schools, and this negative effect generalizes across socioeconomic status and self-regulatory characteristics.
claim.statement_location: Abstract and Conclusion sections (pages 1 and 40 of the PDF)

data.source: OECD Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) 2003 database
data.sample_size: 265,180 students (after excluding missing data and very small school samples)
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual students nested within schools and countries
data.access_details: Publicly available upon registration via the OECD PISA data portal
data.notes: Five plausible values for mathematics ability were used; hierarchical structure (students within schools within countries); some missing data removed prior to analysis.

method.description: The study employed three-level multilevel modeling to examine the association between school-average mathematics ability and students’ mathematics self-concept (BFLPE) and to test interactions with numerous potential moderators such as SES indicators, motivation, self-efficacy, learning strategies, anxiety, and social relationship variables.
steps: Download and merge the student- and school-level PISA 2003 data files.
Select 15-year-old students with complete data on mathematics self-concept, mathematics plausible values, and moderator variables; remove schools with very small samples.
Standardize the five plausible values of individual mathematics ability and compute school-average mathematics ability for each school.

For each of the five plausible values, specify a three-level multilevel regression model (students L1, schools L2, countries L3) predicting mathematics self-concept from individual ability, school-average ability, moderator, and interaction between moderator and school-average ability.
Repeat the model for every moderator variable; average coefficients across the five plausible values to obtain final estimates.
Evaluate statistical significance at p < .001 and compute effect sizes analogous to Cohen’s d.
Interpret main BFLPE coefficient and any significant interaction effects.
method.models: Three-level hierarchical linear models with cross-level interactions and fixed and random effects
method.independent_variables: School-average mathematics ability (primary predictor), individual mathematics ability, moderators (SES indicators, intrinsic/extrinsic motivation, self-efficacy, learning strategies, anxiety, cooperative and competitive orientations, student-teacher relations, sense of belonging) and their interactions with school-average ability

results.summary: Multilevel analyses confirmed a substantial negative association between school-average mathematics ability and students’ mathematics self-concept, evidencing a robust BFLPE. Individual ability and many personal characteristics positively predicted self-concept, but most interactions with school-average ability were negligible. Moderation effects were statistically significant yet small; memorization strategy and anxiety slightly amplified the BFLPE, whereas factors such as SES indicators, motivation, and self-efficacy showed minimal practical moderation.
